pathophysiology_summary: Guttate psoriasis is a T-cell-mediated eruption of small teardrop-shaped scaly papules over the trunk and proximal limbs, classically triggered 1-3 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis (molecular mimicry). It is benign, non-contagious and self-limiting over weeks, and is unrelated to the penicillin the patient took. The dramatic sudden spread after antibiotics understandably raises fear of a drug allergy or meningococcaemia; both are excluded here by the blanching nature of the rash (negative glass test), the absence of anaphylaxis features (no angioedema/wheeze/urticaria) and a well, afebrile patient. These reassuring features are demonstrable over video.

differential.evidence_basis: Teardrop scaly papules on the trunk 1-2 weeks after strep throat, with a family history of psoriasis.
Recent penicillin prompts screening for urticaria/angioedema/wheeze; absent here.
A widespread rash mandates excluding a non-blanching purpuric rash with fever/toxicity.
Considered, but no herald patch and the post-strep timeline favours guttate psoriasis.
No anaphylaxis features (DI-001), blanching rash in an afebrile well patient (DI-002), classic post-strep teardrop papules with a psoriasis family history (DI-003) — benign guttate psoriasis.
No urticaria, angioedema, wheeze or hypotension — not anaphylaxis.
Blanching rash, afebrile and well — meningococcaemia excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure benign post-strep guttate psoriasis (not a drug allergy, not contagious), emollients + mild topical steroid, routine GP/dermatology follow-up, with anaphylaxis and non-blanching-fever safety-nets. ED escalation is over-triage.

red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed meningococcaemia progresses to septic shock.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing a post-antibiotic rash to penicillin allergy without noting the blanching, post-strep teardrop pattern — risking an unwarranted lifelong penicillin allergy label.
Sending a well, afebrile patient with a blanching rash to ED for presumed anaphylaxis/meningococcaemia.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal dermatological examination and, if needed, skin biopsy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure benign guttate psoriasis with emollient/topical steroid and routine follow-up; anaphylaxis and non-blanching-fever safety-nets.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: A wrongful penicillin-allergy label has long-term consequences; over-triage wastes emergency resources; but missing a genuine non-blanching febrile rash would be dangerous — hence the retained safety-nets.
